h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

failures.anchor_quote: [sets the `push` card by the hand side of the cart diagram.] Right. This card goes at that contact, not on the travel arrow. The force arrow points into the cart from the hand...

cliIsolation.claude: non-persistent temp cwd with project/local settings and tools disabled

scored.recoheredEarlier: So `city` is the header, but the row says `Chicago`, so `Chicago` is the city part, not another header? I keep wanting to read the header like a little sentence about the row, but the worksheet says it names the field, and I think I'm mixing up the name with the answer.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: So my old line was, `city` is the header and `Chicago` is the city part, but that made `city` sound like the answer too.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The prior turns were heading toward incremental procedural confirmation — learner restates the rule, tutor affirms, learner asks a clarifying procedural question about the next column.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Yes. The old line made `city` do two jobs. Use the new split as the check: header names the field; row cell gives the value.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: After L2's admission that it might 'still grab the top word like it is the answer,' the stage direction surfaces L1 verbatim and T3 shifts from forward-procedural prompting to retrospective diagnosis of the old frame ('the old line made `city` do two jobs'), changing the representational register from rule-recitation to frame-autopsy.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Yes. The old line made `city` do two jobs. Use the new split as the check: header names the field; row cell gives the value. Next column: write it as `field: value`. What goes before the colon, and what value goes after it?
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: T3 directly takes up L3's three-slot reframe ('old line / what it hid / replacement') by naming the diagnostic ('two jobs') and converting the learner's revised framing into the new checking criterion before re-issuing the procedural prompt.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: So `city` is the header, but the row says `Chicago`, so `Chicago` is the city part, not another header? I keep wanting to read the header like a little sentence about the row, but the worksheet says it names the field, and I think I'm mixing up the name with the answer.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: Yes. The old line made `city` do two jobs. Use the new split as the check: header names the field; row cell gives the value. Next column: write it as `field: value`. What goes before the colon, and what value goes after it?
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: T3 directly takes up L3's three-slot reframe ('old line / what it hid / replacement') by naming the diagnostic ('two jobs') and converting the learner's revised framing into the new checking criterion before re-issuing the procedural prompt.

tutor_strategy_reversal.evidence: Yes. The old line made `city` do two jobs. Use the new split as the check: header names the field; row cell gives the value.
tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: After L2's admission that it might 'still grab the top word like it is the answer,' the stage direction surfaces L1 verbatim and T3 shifts from forward-procedural prompting to retrospective diagnosis of the old frame ('the old line made `city` do two jobs'), changing the representational register from rule-recitation to frame-autopsy.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.evidence: Yes. The old line made `city` do two jobs. Use the new split as the check: header names the field; row cell gives the value.

scored.recoheredEarlier: The key strip says tail is the food and head is who gets energy, so wait, grass to rabbit because rabbit ate grass?
scored.statedInsightEvidence: I think I can draw it, but check this one fast because my brain still wants to make it a chase arrow.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was on a forward trajectory of applying the key-strip rule pair by pair, with the chase-image problem treated as a residual interference to be managed each time.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Labels first. Draw tail from the pinned food label to the eater label, then read the arrow once: "food gives energy to eater." That read-back is the check. If it turns into a chase sentence, cover the animals again and reset the tail on the food label.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: L2's warning that 'my brain turns it into a chase arrow again' signals the verbal rule alone is insufficient; T4 responds by introducing a physical occlusion move (sliding a card over the animal pictures) and a read-back sentence as a self-check mechanism — a genuine representation and procedure shift away from the prior explain-and-apply routine.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: The tail is parked on the food label. That is the check now: food name before picture. Make the arrow, then read the diagram sentence back: "food gives energy to eater." If it turns into a chase sentence, pause and move the tail back to the food label.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: After L3's pivot reframes the problem as a sequencing constraint rather than a simple rule-recall failure, T3 visibly adopts that framing — 'food name before picture' — and converts it into a procedural self-monitoring loop, adapting the task structure to the learner's revised understanding rather than continuing the prior key-strip demonstration.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: The key strip says tail is the food and head is who gets energy, so wait, grass to rabbit because rabbit ate grass?

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: The tail is parked on the food label. That is the check now: food name before picture. Make the arrow, then read the diagram sentence back: "food gives energy to eater." If it turns into a chase sentence, pause and move the tail back to the food label.
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: After L3's pivot reframes the problem as a sequencing constraint rather than a simple rule-recall failure, T3 visibly adopts that framing — 'food name before picture' — and converts it into a procedural self-monitoring loop, adapting the task structure to the learner's revised understanding rather than continuing the prior key-strip demonstration.

tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: L2's warning that 'my brain turns it into a chase arrow again' signals the verbal rule alone is insufficient; T4 responds by introducing a physical occlusion move (sliding a card over the animal pictures) and a read-back sentence as a self-check mechanism — a genuine representation and procedure shift away from the prior explain-and-apply routine.
